[Ahmadiyya](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") is considered distinct from mainstream Islam. In its writing, the present age has been witness to the evil of man and wrath of God, with war and natural disaster.[[90]](#cite_note-96) [Ghulam Ahmad](/wiki/Mirza_Ghulam_Ahmad "Mirza Ghulam Ahmad") is seen as the [promised Messiah](/wiki/Second_coming "Second coming") and the [Mahdi](/wiki/Mahdi "Mahdi"), fulfilling Islamic and Biblical prophecies, as well as scriptures of other religions such as Hinduism. His teaching will establish spiritual reform and establish an age of peace. This will continue for a thousand years, and will unify mankind under one faith.[[91]](#cite_note-97)

Ahmadis believe that despite harsh and strong opposition and discrimination they will eventually be triumphant and their message vindicated both by Muslims and non-Muslims alike. Ahmadis also incorporate the eschatological views from other religions into their doctrine and believe Mirza Ghulam Ahmed falls into this sequence.[[92]](#cite_note-98)

### Baháʼí Faith

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Baháʼí Faith")]

See also: [Baháʼí Faith on life after death](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith_on_life_after_death "Baháʼí Faith on life after death") and [Baháʼí teachings](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_teachings "Baháʼí teachings")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Lotus_Temple%2C_located_in_Delhi%2C_India%2C_is_a_Bah%C3%A1%27%C3%AD_House_of_Worship.jpg/250px-Lotus_Temple%2C_located_in_Delhi%2C_India%2C_is_a_Bah%C3%A1%27%C3%AD_House_of_Worship.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lotus_Temple,_located_in_Delhi,_India,_is_a_Bah%C3%A1%27%C3%AD_House_of_Worship.jpg)

Bahá'í House of Worship, Delhi, India

In the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith"), creation has neither a beginning nor an end;[[93]](#cite_note-99) Baháʼís regard the eschatologies of other religions as symbolic. In Baháʼí belief, human time is marked by a series of [progressive revelations](/wiki/Progressive_revelation_(Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD) "Progressive revelation (Baháʼí)") in which successive [messengers](/wiki/Manifestation_of_God_(Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith) "Manifestation of God (Baháʼí Faith)") or prophets come from God.[[94]](#cite_note-PSmith-100) The coming of each of these messengers is seen as the day of judgment to the adherents of the previous religion, who may choose to accept the new messenger and enter the "heaven" of belief, or denounce the new messenger and enter the "hell" of denial. In this view, the terms "heaven" and "hell" become symbolic terms for a person's spiritual progress and their nearness to or distance from God.[[94]](#cite_note-PSmith-100) In Baháʼí belief, [Bahá'u'lláh](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%27u%27ll%C3%A1h "Bahá'u'lláh") (1817–1892), the founder of the Baháʼí Faith, was the [Second Coming](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming") of Christ and also the fulfilment of previous eschatological expectations of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and other major religions.[[95]](#cite_note-101)

The inception of the Baháʼí Faith coincides with [Great Disappointment](/wiki/Great_Disappointment "Great Disappointment") of the Millerite prophecy in 1844.

[ʻAbdu'l-Bahá](/wiki/%CA%BBAbdu%27l-Bah%C3%A1 "ʻAbdu'l-Bahá") taught that Armageddon would begin in 1914,[[96]](#cite_note-102) but without a clear indication of its end date.[[97]](#cite_note-103) Baháʼís believe that the mass martyrdom anticipated during the *End Times* had already passed within the [historical context](/wiki/Persecution_of_Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%ADs#Historical_context "Persecution of Baháʼís") of the Baháʼí Faith.[[98]](#cite_note-104)[[99]](#cite_note-105) Baháʼís expect their faith to be eventually embraced by the masses of the world, ushering in a [golden age](/wiki/Golden_age "Golden age").

### Rastafari

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Rastafari")]

Main article: [Rastafari movement](/wiki/Rastafari_movement "Rastafari movement")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Selassie_restored.jpg/250px-Selassie_restored.jpg)](/wiki/File:Selassie_restored.jpg)

[Haile Selassie I](/wiki/Haile_Selassie_I "Haile Selassie I") is viewed as god incarnate in [Rastafari](/wiki/Rastafari "Rastafari").

Rastafari have a unique interpretation of end times, based on the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") and the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation"). They believe [Ethiopian](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia") Emperor [Haile Selassie I](/wiki/Haile_Selassie_I "Haile Selassie I") to be God [incarnate](/wiki/Incarnate "Incarnate"), the *King of kings* and *Lord of lords* mentioned in Revelation 5:5. They saw the crowning of Selassie as the [second coming](/wiki/Second_coming "Second coming"), and the [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War") as fulfillment of Revelation. There is also the expectation that Selassie will return for a day of judgment and bring home the "lost children of Israel", which in Rastafari refers to those taken from Africa through the [slave trade](/wiki/History_of_slavery "History of slavery"). There will then be an era of peace and harmony at Mount [Zion](/wiki/Zion "Zion") in Africa.[[100]](#cite_note-Brown-106)

## Cyclic cosmology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Cyclic cosmology")]

### Hinduism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Hinduism")]

Main articles: [Hindu eschatology](/wiki/Hindu_eschatology "Hindu eschatology") and [Kalki](/wiki/Kalki "Kalki")

Further information: [Hindu units of time](/wiki/Hindu_units_of_time "Hindu units of time")

The [Vaishnavite](/wiki/Vaishnavite "Vaishnavite") tradition links contemporary Hindu eschatology to the figure of [Kalki](/wiki/Kalki "Kalki"), the tenth and last [avatar](/wiki/Avatar "Avatar") of [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu"). Many Hindus believe that before [the age](/wiki/Hindu_units_of_time "Hindu units of time") draws to a close, Kalki will reincarnate as [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") and simultaneously dissolve and regenerate the universe. [Shaivites](/wiki/Shaivism "Shaivism") hold the view that Shiva is incessantly destroying and creating the world.[[101]](#cite_note-107)

In [Hindu eschatology](/wiki/Hindu_eschatology "Hindu eschatology"), time is cyclic and consists of [kalpas](/wiki/Kalpa_(aeon) "Kalpa (aeon)"). Each lasts 4.1–8.2 billion years, which is a period of one full day and night for [Brahma](/wiki/Brahma "Brahma"), who will be alive for 311 trillion, 40 billion years. Within a *kalpa* there are periods of [creation](/wiki/Brahma "Brahma"), [preservation](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu") and [decline](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva"). After this larger cycle, all of creation will contract to a [singularity](/wiki/Gravitational_singularity "Gravitational singularity")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and then again will expand from that single point, as the [ages](/wiki/Yuga "Yuga") continue in a religious [fractal](/wiki/Fractal "Fractal") pattern.[[77]](#cite_note-Hooper-77)[*[need quotation to verify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Within the current kalpa, there are four epochs that encompass the cycle. They progress from a beginning of complete purity to a descent into total corruption. The last of the four ages is [Kali Yuga](/wiki/Kali_Yuga "Kali Yuga") (which most Hindus believe is the current time), characterized by quarrel, hypocrisy, impiety, violence and decay. The four pillars of dharma will be reduced to one, with truth being all that remains.[[102]](#cite_note-108) As written in the [Gita](/wiki/Bhagavad_Gita "Bhagavad Gita"):[[103]](#cite_note-109)

> *Yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānirbhavati Bhārata*  
> *Abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānam sṛjāmyaham*  
>   
> O descendant of Bharata, whenever there is a decline of religion and an increase in irreligion, at that time I manifest My eternally perfect form in this mundane world.

At this time of chaos, the final avatar, [Kalki](/wiki/Kalki "Kalki"), endowed with eight superhuman faculties will appear on a white horse. Kalki will amass an army to "establish righteousness upon the earth" and leave "the minds of the people as pure as crystal."

At the completion of [Kali Yuga](/wiki/Kali_Yuga "Kali Yuga"), the next [Yuga Cycle](/wiki/Yuga_Cycle "Yuga Cycle") will begin with a new [Satya Yuga](/wiki/Satya_Yuga "Satya Yuga"), in which all will once again be righteous with the reestablishment of dharma. This, in turn, will be followed by epochs of [Treta Yuga](/wiki/Treta_Yuga "Treta Yuga"), [Dvapara Yuga](/wiki/Dvapara_Yuga "Dvapara Yuga") and again another Kali Yuga. This cycle will then repeat until the larger cycle of existence under [Brahma](/wiki/Brahma "Brahma") returns to the [singularity](/wiki/Gravitational_singularity "Gravitational singularity"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and a new universe is born.[[100]](#cite_note-Brown-106)
The cycle of [birth](/wiki/Brahma "Brahma"), [growth](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu"), [decay](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva"), and renewal at the individual level finds its echo in the cosmic order, yet is affected by vagueries of divine intervention in Vaishnavite belief.

### Buddhism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Buddhism")]

Main article: [Buddhist eschatology](/wiki/Buddhist_eschatology "Buddhist eschatology")

There is no classic account of beginning or end[[104]](#cite_note-110)
in Buddhism; Masao Abe attributes this to the absence of God.[[105]](#cite_note-111)

History is embedded in the continuing process of [samsara](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83s%C4%81ra "Saṃsāra") or the "beginningless and endless cycles of birth-death-rebirth".[[106]](#cite_note-112) Buddhists believe there is an end to things[[107]](#cite_note-113) but it is not final because they are bound to be born again. However, the writers of [Mahayana](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") Buddhist scriptures establish a specific end-time account in Buddhist tradition: this describes the return of [Maitreya Buddha](/wiki/Maitreya_Buddha "Maitreya Buddha"), who would bring about an end to the world.[[108]](#cite_note-114) This constitutes one of the two major branches of Buddhist eschatology, with the other being the [Sermon of the Seven Suns](/wiki/Sermon_of_the_seven_suns "Sermon of the seven suns"). End time in Buddhism could also involve a cultural eschatology covering "final things", which include the idea that [Sakyamuni Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha")'s [dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma") will also come to an end.[[109]](#cite_note-115)

#### Maitreya

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Maitreya")]

Main article: [Maitreya](/wiki/Maitreya "Maitreya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Astasahasrika_Prajnaparamita_Maitreya_Detail.jpeg/250px-Astasahasrika_Prajnaparamita_Maitreya_Detail.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Astasahasrika_Prajnaparamita_Maitreya_Detail.jpeg)

Bodhisattva Maitreya

The Buddha described his teachings disappearing five thousand years from when he preached them,[[110]](#cite_note-116) corresponding approximately to the year 4300 since he was born in 623 BCE.[[111]](#cite_note-117) At this time, knowledge of dharma will be lost as well. The last of his relics will be gathered in Bodh Gaya and cremated.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There will be a new era in which the next Buddha Maitreya will appear, but it will be preceded by the degeneration of human society. This will be a period of greed, lust, poverty, ill will, violence, murder, impiety, physical weakness, sexual depravity and societal collapse, and even the Buddha himself will be forgotten.[[77]](#cite_note-Hooper-77)

This will be followed by the coming of Maitreya when the teachings of [dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma") are forgotten. Maitreya was the first Bodhisattva around whom a cult developed, in approximately the third century CE.[[112]](#cite_note-Britannica-118)

The earliest known mention of Maitreya occurs in the Cakkavatti, or Sihanada Sutta in [Digha Nikaya](/wiki/Digha_Nikaya "Digha Nikaya") 26 of the [Pali Canon](/wiki/Pali_Canon "Pali Canon"). In it, [Gautama Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha") predicted his teachings of dharma would be forgotten after 5,000 years.

> At that period, brethren, there will arise in the world an Exalted One named Maitreya, Fully Awakened, abounding in wisdom and goodness, happy, with knowledge of the worlds, unsurpassed as a guide to mortals willing to be led, a teacher for gods and men, an Exalted One, a Buddha, even as I am now. He, by himself, will thoroughly know and see, as it were face to face, this universe, with Its worlds of the spirits, Its Brahmas and Its Maras, and Its world of recluses and Brahmins, of princes and peoples, even as I now, by myself, thoroughly know and see them.

— Digha Nikaya, 26

The text then foretells the birth of Maitreya Buddha in the city of [Ketumatī](/wiki/Ketumat%C4%AB "Ketumatī") in present-day [Benares](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi"), whose king will be the Cakkavattī Sankha. Sankha will live in the former palace of King Mahāpanadā, and will become a renunciate who follows Maitreya.[[113]](#cite_note-119)[[114]](#cite_note-120)

In Mahayana Buddhism, Maitreya will attain *[bodhi](/wiki/Bodhi "Bodhi")* in seven days, the minimum period, by virtue of his many lifetimes of preparation. Once Buddha, he will rule over the Ketumati Pure Land, an earthly paradise sometimes associated with the Indian city of [Varanasi](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi") or Benares in present-day [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh"). In Mahayana Buddhism, the Buddha presides over a land of purity. For example, [Amitabha](/wiki/Amitabha "Amitabha") presides over Sukhavati, more popularly known as the "Western Paradise".[[115]](#cite_note-121)

A notable teaching he will rediscover is that of the ten non-virtuous deeds—killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisive speech, abusive speech, idle speech, covetousness, harmful intent and wrong views. The ten virtuous deeds will replace them with the abandonment of each of these practices. [Edward Conze](/wiki/Edward_Conze "Edward Conze") in his *Buddhist Scriptures* (1959) gives an account of Maitreya:

> The Lord replied, 'Maitreya, the best of men, will then leave the [*Tuṣita*](/wiki/Tushita "Tushita") heavens and go for his last rebirth. As soon as he is born he will walk seven steps forward, and where he puts down his feet a jewel or a lotus will spring up. He will raise his eyes to the ten directions and will speak these words: "This is my last birth. There will be no rebirth after this one. Never will I come back here, but, all pure, I shall win Nirvana."'

— *Buddhist Scriptures*[[116]](#cite_note-122)

Maitreya currently resides in [Tushita](/wiki/Tushita "Tushita"), but will come to [Jambudvipa](/wiki/Jambudvipa "Jambudvipa") when needed most as successor to the historic [Śākyamuni Buddha](/wiki/%C5%9A%C4%81kyamuni_Buddha "Śākyamuni Buddha"). Maitreya will achieve complete enlightenment during his lifetime, and following this reawakening he will bring back the timeless teaching of [dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma") to this plane and rediscover [enlightenment](/wiki/Nirvana_(concept) "Nirvana (concept)").[[117]](#cite_note-Theravada_Buddhism_1988,_pages_83-85-123) The [Arya Maitreya Mandala](/wiki/Arya_Maitreya_Mandala "Arya Maitreya Mandala"), founded in 1933 by [Lama Anagarika Govinda](/wiki/Lama_Anagarika_Govinda "Lama Anagarika Govinda"), is based on the idea of Maitreya.

Maitreya eschatology forms the central canon of the [White Lotus Society](/wiki/White_Lotus_Societies "White Lotus Societies"), a religious and political movement which emerged in [Yuan China](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty").[[118]](#cite_note-124) It later branched into the Chinese underground [criminal organization](/wiki/Organized_crime "Organized crime") known as the [Triads](/wiki/Triad_(organized_crime) "Triad (organized crime)"), which exist today as an international underground criminal network. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Note that no description of Maitreya occurs in any other sutta in the canon, casting doubt as to the authenticity of the scripture. In addition, sermons of the Buddha normally are in response to a question, or in a specific context, but this sutta has a beginning and an ending, and its content is quite different from the others. This has led some to conclude that the whole sutta is [apocryphal](/wiki/Apocrypha "Apocrypha"), or tampered with.[[117]](#cite_note-Theravada_Buddhism_1988,_pages_83-85-123)

#### Sermon of the Seven Suns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sermon of the Seven Suns")]

In his "Sermon of the Seven Suns" in the [Pali Canon](/wiki/Pali_Canon "Pali Canon"), the Buddha describes the ultimate fate of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") in an apocalypse characterized by the consequent appearance of seven [suns](/wiki/Sun "Sun") in the sky, each causing progressive ruin until the planet is destroyed:

> All things are impermanent, all aspects of existence are unstable and non-eternal. Beings will become so weary and disgusted with the constituent things that they will seek emancipation from them more quickly. There will come a season, O monks when, after hundreds of thousands of years, rains will cease. All seedlings, all vegetation, all plants, grasses and trees will dry up and cease to be. ...There comes another season after a great lapse of time when a second sun will appear. Now all brooks and ponds will dry up, vanish, cease to be.

— Aňguttara-Nikăya, VII, 6.2 Pali Canon[[77]](#cite_note-Hooper-77)

The canon goes on to describe the progressive destruction of each sun. The third sun will dry the [Ganges River](/wiki/Ganges_River "Ganges River") and other rivers, whilst the fourth will cause the lakes to evaporate; the fifth will dry the oceans. Later:

> Again after a vast period of time a sixth sun will appear, and it will bake the Earth even as a pot is baked by a potter. All the mountains will reek and send up clouds of smoke. After another great interval a seventh sun will appear and the Earth will blaze with fire until it becomes one mass of flame. The mountains will be consumed, a spark will be carried on the wind and go to the worlds of God. ...Thus, monks, all things will burn, perish and exist no more except those who have seen the path.

— Aňguttara-Nikăya, VII, 6.2 Pali Canon[[77]](#cite_note-Hooper-77)

The sermon completes with the Earth immersed into an extensive holocaust. The Pali Canon does not indicate when this will happen relative to Maitreya.[[77]](#cite_note-Hooper-77)

### Norse mythology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Norse mythology")]

Main article: [Ragnarök](/wiki/Ragnar%C3%B6k "Ragnarök")

1905 Paintings by [Emil Doepler](/wiki/Emil_Doepler "Emil Doepler")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Odin_und_Fenriswolf_Freyr_und_Surt.jpg/250px-Odin_und_Fenriswolf_Freyr_und_Surt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Odin_und_Fenriswolf_Freyr_und_Surt.jpg)

[Odin](/wiki/Odin "Odin") fighting his old nemesis [Fenrir](/wiki/Fenrir "Fenrir")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Ragnar%C3%B6k_by_Doepler.jpg/250px-Ragnar%C3%B6k_by_Doepler.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ragnar%C3%B6k_by_Doepler.jpg)

[Ragnarök](/wiki/Ragnar%C3%B6k "Ragnarök") after [Surtr](/wiki/Surtr "Surtr") has engulfed the world with fire

[Norse mythology](/wiki/Norse_mythology "Norse mythology") depicts the end of days as *[Ragnarök](/wiki/Ragnar%C3%B6k "Ragnarök")*, an [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") term translatable as "twilight of the gods". It will be heralded by a devastation known as [Fimbulvetr](/wiki/Fimbulvetr "Fimbulvetr") which will seize [Midgard](/wiki/Midgard "Midgard") in cold and darkness. The sun and moon will disappear from the sky, and poison will fill the air. The dead will rise from the ground and there will be widespread despair.

Then there will be a battle between—on the one hand—the Gods with the [Æsir](/wiki/%C3%86sir "Æsir"), [Vanir](/wiki/Vanir "Vanir")[[119]](#cite_note-125) and [Einherjar](/wiki/Einherjar "Einherjar"), led by [Odin](/wiki/Odin "Odin"), and—on the other hand—forces of Chaos, including the fire giants and [jötunn](/wiki/J%C3%B6tunn "Jötunn"), led by [Loki](/wiki/Loki "Loki"). In the fighting Odin will be swallowed whole by his old nemesis [Fenrir](/wiki/Fenrir "Fenrir").[[120]](#cite_note-LARRINGTON266-126) The god [Freyr](/wiki/Freyr "Freyr") fights [Surtr](/wiki/Surtr "Surtr") but loses. [Víðarr](/wiki/V%C3%AD%C3%B0arr "Víðarr"), son of Odin, will then avenge his father by ripping Fenrir's jaws apart and stabbing the wolf in the heart with his spear. The serpent [Jörmungandr](/wiki/J%C3%B6rmungandr "Jörmungandr") will open its gaping maw and be met in combat by [Thor](/wiki/Thor "Thor"). Thor, also a son of Odin, will defeat the serpent, only to take nine steps afterwards before collapsing in his own death.[[121]](#cite_note-127)

After this people will flee their homes as the sun blackens and the earth sinks into the sea. The stars will vanish, steam will rise, and flames will touch the heavens. This conflict will result in the deaths of most of the major Gods and forces of Chaos. Finally, Surtr will fling fire across the nine worlds. The ocean will then completely submerge [Midgard](/wiki/Midgard "Midgard").[[122]](#cite_note-128)

After the [cataclysm](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/cataclysm "wikt:cataclysm"), the world will resurface new and fertile, and the surviving Gods will meet. [Baldr](/wiki/Baldr "Baldr"), another son of Odin, will be reborn in the new world, according to [Völuspá](/wiki/V%C3%B6lusp%C3%A1 "Völuspá"). The two human survivors, [Líf and Lífþrasir](/wiki/L%C3%ADf_and_L%C3%ADf%C3%BErasir "Líf and Lífþrasir"), will then repopulate this new earth.[[123]](#cite_note-Odin-129)

### Egyptian mythology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Egyptian mythology")]

Main article: [Egyptian mythology § End of the universe](/wiki/Egyptian_mythology#End_of_the_universe "Egyptian mythology")

Egyptian texts typically treat the dissolution of the world as a possibility to be avoided, and for that reason they do not often describe it in detail. However, many texts allude to the idea that the world, after countless cycles of renewal, is destined to end. This end is described in a passage in the *Coffin Texts* and a more explicit one in the *Book of the Dead*, in which Atum says that he will one day dissolve the ordered world and return to his primeval, inert state within the waters of chaos. All things other than the creator will cease to exist, except Osiris, who will survive along with him.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHornung1982162–165-130) Details about this **eschatological** prospect are left unclear, including the fate of the dead who are associated with Osiris.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunandZivie-Coche200467–68-131) Yet with the creator god and the god of renewal together in the waters that gave rise to the orderly world, there is the potential for a new creation to arise in the same manner as the old.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeeksFavard-Meeks199618–19-132)

### Taoism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Taoism")]

Main article: [Taoist eschatology](/wiki/Li_Hong_(Taoist_eschatology) "Li Hong (Taoist eschatology)")

The Taoist faith is not concerned with what came before or after life, knowing only their own being in the Tao. The philosophy is that people come and go, just like mountains, trees and stars, but Tao will go on for [time immemorial](/wiki/Time_immemorial "Time immemorial").[[127]](#cite_note-133)

## Analogies in science and philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eschatology&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Analogies in science and philosophy")]

Further information: [Global catastrophe scenarios](/wiki/Global_catastrophe_scenarios "Global catastrophe scenarios"), [Global catastrophic risk](/wiki/Global_catastrophic_risk "Global catastrophic risk"), [End of history](/wiki/End_of_history "End of history"), and [Accelerationism](/wiki/Accelerationism "Accelerationism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Solar_Life_Cycle.svg/250px-Solar_Life_Cycle.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Solar_Life_Cycle.svg)

A diagram showing the life cycle of the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun")

Researchers in [futures studies](/wiki/Futures_studies "Futures studies") and [transhumanists](/wiki/Transhumanists "Transhumanists") investigate how the accelerating rate of scientific progress may lead to a "[technological singularity](/wiki/Technological_singularity "Technological singularity")" in the future that would profoundly and unpredictably change the course of human history, and result in *Homo sapiens* no longer being the dominant life form on Earth.[[128]](#cite_note-agi-conf-134)[[129]](#cite_note-hplusmagazine-135)

Occasionally the term "physical eschatology" is applied to the long-term predictions of astrophysics about the [future of Earth](/wiki/Future_of_Earth "Future of Earth") and [ultimate fate of the universe](/wiki/Ultimate_fate_of_the_universe "Ultimate fate of the universe").[[130]](#cite_note-136)[[131]](#cite_note-137) In approximately 6 billion years, the [Sun](/wiki/Sun#Life_phases "Sun") will turn into a [red giant](/wiki/Red_giant "Red giant"). Life on Earth will become impossible due to a rise in temperature long before the planet is possibly actually swallowed up by the Sun or left charred.[[132]](#cite_note-zeilik-138) Later, the Sun will become a [white dwarf](/wiki/White_dwarf "White dwarf").
